point_support.reason: The U.S. civilian economy grew during WWII, unlike other countries whose economies shrank [ndf0bd955:chunk_00017].
The U.S. emerged from WWII triumphant, contrasting with the defeat of Germany [ndf0bd955:chunk_00013].
The Depression discredited political regimes in both the U.S. and Germany, leading to new leadership [ndf0bd955:chunk_00002].
The U.S. paid a lower price in blood and treasure during WWII compared to Britain and the Soviet Union [ndf0bd955:chunk_00016].
The U.S. economy expanded by 15% during WWII, setting up postwar prosperity [ndf0bd955:chunk_00018].
Post-WWII, the U.S. experienced unmatched prosperity and military superiority [nda91043d:chunk_00075].
The U.S. economy was centralized and coordinated by the federal government during WWI, setting precedents for future crises [nd87f4bcd:chunk_00018].
The U.S. emerged from WWII with its cities and infrastructure intact, unlike Germany and Japan [nda91043d:chunk_00074].
The U.S. economy was heavily involved in international trade, with significant loans to Britain and reduced trade with Germany due to blockades [nd87f4bcd:chunk_00005].
American industries like steel and petroleum profited greatly by selling war-related goods to the government during WWI [nd87f4bcd:chunk_00021].
